artifacts.content:
Hi Danny,

I’m still wired from tonight and needed to write this out before I try to sleep.

Council went until almost 11 p.m. with the Mixed-Use Village hearings. They ended up pushing through that emergency zoning text amendment so we can temporarily waive the standard 15% inclusionary requirement and carve out 20 units for rapid rehousing of the Willow Court families after the meth lab explosion by East Riverbend Elementary. I’m exhausted, but honestly relieved there’s finally a path for those folks. You should’ve seen the parents who came to speak – some of their kids are in Mateo’s grade.

At the same time, I’m bracing for the next round. On April 9 they’re scheduled to adopt the special needs housing overlay and Reasonable Accommodation ordinance so we can do that 12‑bed supportive housing wing tied to the Butte County Behavioral Health court program. I support it 100%, but I can already hear the NIMBY comments about “those people” living near the park and the community center. Part of me wants to stand up and say, “My own family has struggled too,” but I know I can’t make it about us.

All of this is layered on top of the settlement-driven vesting tentative map the Planning Commission approved on June 12 last year to implement the March 2024 settlement with Riverbend Equity Partners after their 2022 denial over the old Riverbend Plating Works lead mess. I still have to dance around that in every public explanation.

Meanwhile, normal life goes on: Izzy has a science project due Friday, Mateo’s AYSO coach keeps texting about field space, and your mom called again about coming over to make caldo on Thursday. Can we trade – you handle science fair triage and I’ll do bedtime?

Heading to bed now. I’ll probably be up at 5:30 anyway, walking my loop with a podcast.

Love,
Laura

metadata.subject: After the Willow Court and overlay hearings

artifacts.content:
Planner’s journal – personal (not for sharing)

I keep thinking about how tangled my work on the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village has become with family stuff.

Rosa: Since her mild stroke in March 2022 and then the cardiologist at Enloe telling her in May 2024 to stop driving, my weekends are a blur of bus rides and bike hops between Riverbend and Chico. Every other Saturday I leave from the RABA stop by 250 Civic Center Plaza, transfer in Chico, then bike the path to her apartment, haul groceries, and sit through appointments. It’s exactly why I’m so insistent that the Class I multi-use path and transit stop upgrades get built early.

Mateo: His asthma has been flaring again. At the March 2024 visit with Dr. Karen Liu at Riverbend Children’s Clinic, she told us to keep him indoors on days when PM2.5 spikes during grading and truck hauling on Oak Terrace and Riverside Drive near East Riverbend Elementary. Now every time I review the construction-phase air quality mitigation for the creek grading, I’m picturing him wheezing in the nurse’s office.

Izzy: She still tenses up at the Cypress & 5th and East Riverbend Boulevard crossings after that November 2022 near-sideswipe and the later near-miss that pushed us into counseling at Riverbend Family Wellness Center starting February 2024. I’ve basically turned into a human Safe Routes to School checklist.

Note to self: keep using these personal experiences to push for better dust control, quieter nighttime work near Willow Court seniors, and genuinely safe bike/ped routes – but don’t drag the family’s medical or mental health details into official language.

metadata.title: Journal – family threads in the Village project

artifacts.content:
Hi Javi,

Catching up between meetings and thought I’d answer your last message properly.

On your South 7th Street shop: after our June–August 2023 right-of-way negotiation saga, Mark Feldman pulled me aside and said I had to formally recuse myself from anything involving your parcel. Once he realized your 2019–2021 Franchise Tax Board back-tax payment plan might get dragged into the relocation benefits review, it was a hard line. I filed a recusal memo and now I literally leave the room when your property comes up. I can still help you as your sister, just not as “the City.”

Same story with the HVAC expansion idea at 1025 East Riverbend Boulevard. The land behind you is still AG‑20 with an Agriculture/Open Space General Plan designation, same as the 118.5-acre pasture. When Karen Liu raised your email about me being able to “loosen up the ag zoning” in the April 18, 2024 pre-app meeting, I stepped out and told her in writing that I can’t touch any variance or rezone for you.

Mom’s in a similar gray zone. She keeps asking if we can just rezone the 2.5‑acre piece at 980 County Road 27 to R‑1 so she can sell it and knock down the $48,000 from her Enloe knee replacement. I’ve told her (and you) since spring 2023 it doesn’t work that way, and I definitely can’t be the one spearheading it.

On top of that, Michael Levin reminded me after my panic attack at the October 3, 2024 Planning Commission study session that Daniel and I co-own those old rentals at 214 and 222 East Riverbend Boulevard right across from the pasture. So I’m walking a tightrope on conflicts everywhere.

Anyway, send me your latest numbers and I’ll at least look them over as your big sister, not as “Senior Planner.”

Abrazo,
Laura

metadata.subject: Re: Your shop, mom’s parcel, and my hands being tied

artifacts.content:
Event: Saturday run to Chico – help Mamá
Date: 2024-05-25
Time: 8:30 a.m. – 4:30 p.m.
Location: Start at RABA stop, Civic Center Plaza → Chico transfer → Mamá’s apartment / Enloe Medical Center
Attendees: Laura, Mateo (maybe)

Notes:

– Bus from 250 Civic Center Plaza around 8:45 a.m., bring Clipper card + bike lock. Since her mild stroke last year and the cardiologist at Enloe telling her in May 2024 not to drive anymore, I need to keep making these every-other-weekend trips so she doesn’t feel stranded.

– 10:00 a.m. grocery run with Mamá (Rosa) – focus on lighter stuff she can manage with one hand. Check if she’s still thinking about selling that 2.5-acre piece at 980 County Road 27 to help with the remaining $48,000 from the Enloe knee replacement bills. Gently remind her I can’t “just rezone a strip” from Agriculture/Open Space to R‑1 for her.

– 1:00 p.m. quick stop at Chico Regional Cancer Center to ask about the 3,200 sq ft satellite clinic concept on Parcel 7 in the Village. Do NOT mention her Stage II diagnosis in any pitch; keep it framed as regional access.

– 3:00 p.m. call with Daniel on the bus back re: car logistics. Since we picked up the Subaru in August 2023 and that $620/month payment kicked in, we’re still a one-car family most days, so I’ll need him to grab Izzy from Riverbend Middle and Mateo from East Riverbend Elementary.

– Evening: no laptop if possible. Maybe just review Mateo’s spelling and let Izzy vent about her science project.

metadata.title: Bus to Chico – Mamá / errands / clinic chat

artifacts.content:
Mami,

Solo quería explicarte mejor por qué estoy tan obsesionada con ciertos detalles del proyecto.

Te acuerdas del 9 de enero de 2023, cuando te quedaste casi tres horas atrapada en el carro en la rampa de salida sur de la 99 en East Riverbend durante esa tormenta enorme? Cuando CHP tuvo que desviar los carros por el área que ahora es parte del plan del Mixed-Use Village? Desde entonces no puedo dejar de pensar en drenaje y rutas de evacuación. Por eso estoy peleando tanto por el sendero elevado y el acceso de emergencia en esa parte.

También, Mateo. En marzo de 2024, cuando lo vimos con la neumóloga pediátrica, la Dra. Karen Liu en Riverbend Children’s Clinic, ella dijo que lo mantuviéramos adentro los días que suben los niveles de PM2.5 por el movimiento de tierra y los camiones en Oak Terrace y Riverside Drive cerca de East Riverbend Elementary. Eso me pega doble, como mamá y como planificadora. Estoy metiendo controles de polvo y límites de camiones lo más fuertes que puedo.

Y no es solo ustedes. Mamá Elena en los apartamentos de Willow Court lleva quejándose desde finales de septiembre de 2023 por los vacuums de los camiones y los colados de concreto después de las 9:30 p.m. Dijo que hasta con el nuevo medicamento que le dieron en Chico Behavioral Health Clinic en octubre, la ansiedad y el insomnio le empeoraron. Así que también estoy presionando para que no haya trabajo nocturno ruidoso cerca de los adultos mayores.

A veces siento que todo el proyecto es una lista de sustos familiares convertidos en normas: tus inundaciones, el asma de Mateo, los nervios de Izzy en los cruces. Pero si eso significa que otras familias sufren menos, vale la pena.

Te llamo el sábado para ver cómo sigues.

Con cariño,
Laura

metadata.subject: Por qué estoy tan intensa con el proyecto

artifacts.content:
Personal planning note – housing / equity threads

The Village keeps forcing me to relive our own near-misses.

Spring 2021: Daniel’s unpaid suspension from Riverbend High meant we fell two months behind on rent at the apartment at 250 Civic Center Plaza. I still remember sitting at the kitchen table with the property manager, negotiating that $3,200 payment plan so we wouldn’t get an eviction notice. When I revised the affordability language this January (2024), that’s what I had in my head as I wrote that at least some of the 80 units over commercial need to be reserved for households at or below 80% AMI, with on-site tenant counseling.

August 2023: we bought the Subaru and took on the $620/month loan, which locked us into being a one-car family. That’s part of why I care so much about the Class II bike lanes on East Riverbend Boulevard and the transit stop upgrades; my own commute is bike + bus most days.

Then there’s Mom’s cancer – the weekly 40‑minute drives from Chico to Chico Regional Cancer Center since her Stage II diagnosis in February 2023. Pushing for that 3,200 sq ft satellite clinic on Parcel 7 isn’t abstract; I’m literally trying to shorten her trip and everyone else’s.

Layer on the more sensitive stuff – Daniel’s second DUI and six‑month suspension in October 2023, Izzy’s anxiety after the near-misses at Cypress & 5th and East Riverbend Boulevard, Mateo’s weight and anxiety issues during the 2022–23 school year – and it’s obvious why I keep front-loading parks, trails, and supportive uses in the phasing plan.

Reminder to self: keep the public language clean, but don’t forget these are the real reasons I’m fighting for certain pieces.

metadata.title: Why the Village feels so personal

artifacts.content:
Hey Maya,

Finally answering your “how are you really?” text.

Work first: The Village Specific Plan is still my life. We’re juggling everything from the 6–8 bed transitional group home on that 0.4‑acre parcel at 3120 West Riverbend Avenue (the one tied to the Riverbend Family Justice Center where my mom’s been volunteering since January 2023) to the idea of a 2,500 sq ft Sierra Vista Behavioral Health MAT clinic in Building D along Willow Creek Parkway. I almost justified the clinic by mentioning that Javier’s ex has been in their 12‑month opioid/meth program since March 2024, but I caught myself – way too personal for a staff report.

On the “cool but exhausting” side, I floated the 4,000 sq ft dual-use STEM lab and makerspace on the south edge of Riverbend High’s campus as part of the “other development” package – a day-use lab for Daniel’s physics kids and an evening workforce training space for at-risk youth starting Fall 2026. He’s half excited, half terrified about being voluntold to run it.

Meanwhile, my regular job still exists: early-morning calls about floodplain modeling along Riverbend Creek, arguing with Public Works over raised crosswalks and curb extensions on East Riverbend Boulevard and at County Road 17, and debriefing with the City Attorney about how to talk around that confidential March 2024 settlement with Riverbend Equity Partners and the old Plating Works lead issues.

Home is the usual chaos. Izzy’s in full teenage eye-roll mode, Mateo is counting down to soccer season and asking when “his” park will finally be built, and Daniel’s grading labs on the couch while I answer public comments about groundwater.

I owe you a visit that doesn’t involve me bringing a stack of EIR chapters. Maybe after the next Council hearing?

Besos,
Laura

metadata.subject: Urban planning, but make it personal
